change_summary: EXP-220 major rewrite integrated: FSF geometric derivation conceded (Pearson r=0.9999 verified independently); pivot to ratio-vs-absolute-magnitude critique; Meza -> Kim & Chang (2018) citation swap; Section 1.8 cross-reference updated to current title (The Coordinate Collapse); 2026-08-12 nine-station external test framing added; Kp range 3-4 -> Kp approx 3; refraction-cascade vocabulary removed.
our_argument_summary: WIN-010 verdict remains Std Model Explains (Non-discriminating). The integrated rewrite now correctly concedes the dome's FSF = H(r)/r geometric scaling (independently verified: Pearson r = 0.999922 against dome's nine published FSF values, diff <0.6% at seven stations, -2.17% at SPT, -2.78% at SNK). The surviving critique is that (a) the absolute magnitude at Boulder (-10.9 nT) is imported from the 2017 observation and reused as the 2026 baseline (training-as-test), (b) the Chapman Sq mechanism quantitatively confirmed by Kim & Chang (2018) predicts the signed Z-decrease at BOU's magnetic latitude without any dome parameter, (c) the Kp < 2 2026 validation gate is asymmetric against a Kp approx 3 baseline, and (d) the derivation exists but the magnitude remains post-hoc. The 2026-08-12 nine-station eclipse is now staged as a concrete out-of-sample discriminator.

relevant_files: prior curmudgeon c5 analysis of monitor.py
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome's 2026 formula: delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF. Baseline = -10.9 nT from 2017 BOU observation. Coverage = Besselian-elements eclipse geometry (standard astronomy). FSF = (H(r)/r)/(H(r_BOU)/r_BOU) where H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) and r solves r*tan(lat) = H(r). Independently verified: for Boulder (40.14 deg) r_BOU = 5406 km; for the nine European stations FSF predictions reproduce the dome's published values with Pearson r = 0.999922 and worst-case deviation of 2.78% at SNK (Canary).
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: FSF is genuinely computed from dome geometry (ratio is derivation-valid). The baseline magnitude is hardcoded from the 2017 observation. Net: the station-to-station scaling is a derivation; the absolute magnitude is a calibration borrowed from the event being predicted.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 precondition on 2026 validation (the 2017 baseline underlying this WIN was recorded at Kp approx 3 per c5). Asymmetric evidentiary standard.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None in the integrated text. The FSF-is-geometric concession matches the dome's code; the 'absolute magnitude imported from observation' claim matches raw-text/03-predictions.txt.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py (per c5 validation) compares a hardcoded -10.9 nT target against the fetched INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 minute-averaged Z-component and applies a Kp < 2 gate; the target value is baked in on the same commit as the fetch line per c5.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) Sq-current mechanism predicts the signed Z-decrease at mid-magnetic-latitudes during an eclipse; Kim & Chang (2018, Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2054) provides quantitative confirmation across a 39-eclipse ensemble with Y-component increase and X/Z/F decreases at magnetic latitudes 30-50 deg. BOU sits at magnetic latitude approx 48 deg, inside the Kim & Chang response zone — the standard model predicts both the sign and approximate magnitude of -10.9 nT without dome parameters.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive was publicly available at the time of monitor.py construction; no independent pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue separate from the author's repository has been located. Under the dome's own definition (prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled) the monitor script hardcodes the target on the same commit as the fetch line, so temporal ordering cannot be verified from the repo history. The dome's OpenTimestamps anchoring covers observation commits, not pre-data prediction commits.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF is derived (proved by our own Python reproduction at r = 0.999922), but FSF is a station-to-station scaling ratio, not a derivation of the -10.9 nT absolute magnitude. The magnitude is adopted from the 2017 observation. Per the dome's own formula, delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF — baseline is an observation, coverage is standard astronomy, FSF is geometric. Nothing in this pipeline produces -10.9 nT from first principles. Tag remains false.

holes_found.description: Internal contradiction between paragraph 1 and paragraph 2 of detail_verdict_text. Paragraph 1: 'The dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations; it simply observes that the signal exists and claims it.' Paragraph 2: 'The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF) uses the BOU 2017 -10.9 nT value...' and paragraph 6 explicitly concedes the FSF = H(r)/r derivation is real. A dome defender can quote paragraph 1 alone, cite the formula in 03-predictions.txt, and claim the review is self-contradictory and sloppy. The intended point is 'no first-principles equation deriving the magnitude from dome parameters', but the current wording hands a rhetorical win to the defender. Same issue was flagged on WIN-068 c8 as moderate in the .finding field — WIN-010 now has it in both .finding ('Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation') and paragraph 1 of .detail_verdict_text.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace paragraph 1 sentence 4 with: 'The dome has no first-principles equation that derives the eclipse magnitude from its own parameters; its published formula delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF takes the magnitude as an input from the 2017 observation it is trying to predict.' Mirror the same tightening in the .finding field: replace 'Dome has no eclipse-magnetic coupling equation' with 'Dome has no first-principles magnitude derivation — its FSF formula uses the 2017 observation as its own baseline.'

holes_found.description: Paragraph 7 'clean external test' framing mis-identifies the discriminator. The text says 'the open question is whether 2026 observations track FSF more tightly than they track eclipse shadow coverage (Besselian-elements geometry, the standard-model prediction).' But both the dome formula AND a standard Sq prediction include coverage as a factor — the dome writes delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF; a standard-physics prediction would be roughly baseline * coverage * f(magnetic_latitude). So 'FSF vs coverage' isn't the real discriminator — the discriminator is FSF (H(r)/r, a function of dome disc radius, which depends on geographic latitude) vs a magnetic-latitude scaling function f(mag_lat). The Kim & Chang 30-50 deg magnetic-latitude response curve is the competing prediction, not 'shadow coverage'. A dome defender will correctly note that coverage is already in the dome's own formula and the review has set up a straw discriminator.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite paragraph 7 sentence 2 to frame the discriminator as: 'Because FSF is now confirmed to track dome disc radius (H(r)/r), the open question is whether 2026 residuals — (observed delta_Z) / (baseline * coverage) — track the dome's FSF = H(r)/r profile more tightly than they track a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang (2018)\'s 30-50 deg response zone.' Also consider listing the magnetic latitude for each of the nine stations alongside the FSF — if EBR at geographic 40.8 / mag approx 43 and SNK at geographic 27.8 / mag approx 14 give mag-lat scalings that differ substantially from FSF ordering, the 2026 test has real bite.

holes_found.description: The claim 'any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with the dome's r*tan(theta) = H(r) convention produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form' is asserted in paragraph 3 without demonstration in the reader-facing text. EXP-220's anticipated_objections[1] response says Gaussian, linear, power-law all produce nearly identical ratios over r = 3330-7021 km — that is the load-bearing evidence for this argument. It belongs in the reader-facing text or a footnote, not just in the EXP archive. A dome defender can challenge 'prove it' and the reader has no response on hand. The primary critique (magnitude-anchor + training-as-test + post-hoc) survives without this argument, but the argument is made and the reader can't evaluate it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-sentence empirical footnote at the end of paragraph 3: '(We verified this: re-fitting H(r) to the dome's geometric calibration targets — sun altitude 5,733 km at BOU, moon altitude 2,534 km — with Gaussian, linear, and power-law functional forms and computing H(r)/r ratios for the same nine station radii yields Pearson r > 0.995 against the dome's exponential in all three cases.)' If that recomputation hasn't been done, either do it or drop the 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim and rely on the ratio-vs-magnitude bite alone — which is already decisive.

holes_found.description: Quantitative precision in paragraph 2: 'reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values (0.642-2.075) to better than 0.1% at seven of nine stations and within 1.8% at the worst (Pearson r = 0.9999)'. Independent recomputation in this review (r*tan(lat) = 8537*exp(-r/8619), standard IAGA station latitudes) gives seven stations within 0.6%, SPT at -2.17%, SNK at -2.78%; Pearson r = 0.999922. The Pearson r matches exactly; the deviation percentages differ by roughly 1 percentage point at the worst two stations. This is likely a choice-of-latitude-precision artifact (IAGA lat to 4 dp vs 2 dp, or dome's internal lat table), but because the text commits to specific numbers ('0.1% at seven of nine', '1.8% worst') the number is falsifiable at the quoted precision. A dome defender running the same calculation will find slight mismatches.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a footnote citing the exact latitude source used (e.g., 'per IAGA station registry to 4 dp') and re-run to confirm the quoted 0.1%/1.8% figures reproduce, or (b) soften the precision to 'within ~3% at all nine stations, Pearson r > 0.999'. The argument does not depend on 0.1% specifically — it depends on the match being tight enough to establish FSF is geometric, and Pearson r > 0.999 already does that.

holes_found.description: .finding field overstates Chapman 1933's quantitative reach. Current: 'Chapman ionospheric mechanism (1933) predicts exact signal magnitude and geometry.' Chapman 1933 was semi-quantitative (the detail_verdict_text acknowledges this: 'Chapman (1933) ... first described'; Kim & Chang 2018 is what provides the modern quantitative confirmation). A dome defender can pick 'Chapman 1933 predicts exact signal magnitude' apart: it doesn't — Chapman proposed the mechanism; modern ionospheric models quantify it. Same pattern was flagged on WIN-068 c8 as a minor overclaim in 'entire ensemble' phrasing.
holes_found.recommendation: Change .finding to: 'Non-discriminating: Chapman 1933 + modern Sq models (Kim & Chang 2018) predict the observed signal magnitude and geometry without dome parameters. Dome has no first-principles magnitude derivation; its FSF formula uses the 2017 observation as its own baseline, making the magnitude circularly calibrated.'

holes_found.description: Paragraph 3 asserts that H(r)/r 'is the same form' as 'any alternative disc-geometry model with a comparable height function'. A defender can object that the specific exponential H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) has two parameters fitted to geometric calibration targets (sun altitude, etc.), not to eclipse data — so even if other H(r) forms fit the FSF ratios, the fact that a geometrically-calibrated H(r) fits eclipse data at r = 0.9999 is non-trivial. This point is conceded in EXP-220 anticipated_objections[0] response ('the values are chosen to fit observed sun altitude + calibrated geometric targets') but the user-facing text doesn't surface this nuance — it reads as 'any H(r) would work therefore it's meaningless' which is stronger than the argument supports.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to paragraph 3 after the 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' sentence: 'The dome's specific H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) was fitted to geometric calibration targets (sun altitude, moon altitude) rather than to eclipse data, so the Pearson r = 0.9999 agreement with eclipse FSF is suggestive — but 9 station points against a smooth 2-parameter function is a low evidentiary bar and the same agreement is achievable with other smooth H(r) profiles fitted to the same geometric targets.'

holes_found.description: Paragraph 5 (post-hoc test) says 'we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author'. The dome's OpenTimestamps anchoring is noted only implicitly ('under the dome author's own definition'). A defender will protest: 'my predictions ARE OTS-anchored on Bitcoin blockchain'. The review's honey-trap/canary logic (documented in CLAUDE.md) is that OTS anchors observation commits, not pre-data prediction commits — so even when OTS is attached, it doesn't establish temporal priority of the prediction relative to the data. This nuance should be made explicit here, otherwise a casual reader sees 'no pre-registration' and 'OTS on Bitcoin' in separate places and concludes we ignored it.
holes_found.recommendation: After 'we could not locate a pre-registration of the expected value in a timestamping venue independent of the author', add: 'The dome's OpenTimestamps anchoring (raw-text/03-predictions.txt: "Eclipse predictions anchored to Bitcoin blockchain") timestamps the observation commits, not separate pre-data prediction documents, so it does not establish temporal priority of the -10.9 nT value relative to the INTERMAGNET archive. See the Timestamp Error tab for the full argument.'

holes_found.description: The cross-WIN triple-count paragraph ends with '69 confirmed predictions — three of those claims share one mechanism.' The number 69 is a dome count at one point in time; query: is the current dome WIN count still 69? (curmudgeon.md context line 17 references 67; CLAUDE.md references dome author claiming 69 WINs.) If this number has drifted and we cite 69 here while Part 3 of our review cites a different number elsewhere, we have a consistency problem.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace '69 confirmed predictions' with a generic 'close to seventy claimed WINs' or keep the exact current count in sync with the same number cited in Parts 2b and 3 of our review. Decider should verify the canonical dome count at integration time.

stronger_arguments.description: The new text under-uses Kim & Chang (2018)'s specificity. Kim & Chang found Y-component INCREASES and X/Z/F DECREASES at magnetic latitudes 30-50 deg. BOU sits at magnetic latitude approx 48 deg, squarely inside the response zone. The dome's observed -10.9 nT is in the Z-component — i.e., a decrease — exactly the SIGN the standard Sq mechanism predicts for that magnetic latitude. The text cites Kim & Chang but doesn't land the punch: 'the sign of the observation is specifically what standard Sq predicts at BOU's magnetic latitude'. Adding this converts a general 'Chapman mechanism explains it' argument into a quantitative prediction-match.
sources: Kim & Chang (2018) Advances in Space Research 61:2040-2054

stronger_arguments.description: The 2026-08-12 test could be strengthened with a pre-registration offer. Since FSF has been conceded as geometrically real, the remaining discriminator is 'FSF-pattern vs magnetic-latitude-pattern'. Computing and publishing the magnetic latitude for all nine 2026 stations NOW (before the eclipse) creates a clean pre-registered alternative prediction. If FSF beats mag-lat scaling on residuals, the dome wins a real point; if mag-lat scaling wins, we win cleanly. Currently the text commits to 'update this WIN when the 2026 data arrives' but doesn't pre-register the alternative.

The Kp asymmetry argument could be tightened with a concrete consequence. Current text: 'the model accepts an unsettled-day observation as a confirmed WIN but requires quiet-day conditions for its own future tests.' A defender will say 'so what — stricter future tests are good'. The sharper version: 'if 2026 eclipse day is Kp >= 2 (which the model's own history says is likely — Kp exceeds 2 roughly 50% of days per NOAA climatology), the monitor.py gate voids the test entirely, converting a high-cost prediction into an N/A. The Kp filter acts as a falsifiability ratchet: unsettled data confirms, disturbed data disqualifies.' Make the filter-as-ratchet argument explicit.
sources: NOAA Kp climatology (K3 occurrence approx 25-35% of days)

kernel_of_truth.description: The FSF = H(r)/r station-to-station scaling IS a genuine geometric derivation from dome parameters — the dome is telling the truth about this specific piece of its own math. Pearson r = 0.999922 against the dome's nine published FSF values, verified independently. The dome has also correctly noticed that eclipse-magnetic anomalies exist, that they are electromagnetic rather than gravitational (WIN-013/014 SG nulls), and that they track shadow geometry rather than solar time — all of which are real observational facts.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: A station-to-station ratio is a SCALING LAW, not a MAGNITUDE PREDICTION. The absolute value of -10.9 nT at Boulder is imported from the 2017 observation and reused as the 2026 baseline (training-as-test, explicitly so in the dome's own delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF formula). The scaling shape is also reproducible with other smooth H(r) profiles fitted to the same geometric calibration targets, so the 0.9999 match is to a geometric ratio, not a uniquely dome-geometric discriminator. Finally, the standard Chapman Sq mechanism (Kim & Chang 2018) predicts the signed Z-decrease at BOU's magnetic latitude without any dome parameters — the observation that confirms WIN-010 also confirms the standard model. 'Dome is right about this piece' does not equal 'WIN-010 is a WIN'; it equals 'dome has produced a valid geometric identity that also happens to be non-discriminating'.

deeper_analysis:
EXP-220 is a substantial and scientifically honest rewrite: it reverses a critical factual error from c3/c4 (the 'FSF is empirically fitted' claim, which my c5 review caught and this rewrite corrects), swaps the unverified Meza citation for the already-verified Kim & Chang (2018) anchor that is now shared across WIN-010/025/068, updates the Section 1.8 cross-reference, and sets up the 2026-08-12 eclipse as a concrete external test. The pivot from 'FSF is not derived' to 'FSF is derived as a ratio but the magnitude is still borrowed' is correct, and independently re-verifiable (my own Python reproduction confirms Pearson r = 0.999922). The Advocate-Mode prongs from c5 (Chapman qualitative, similar-r, empirical-tuning) all close successfully.

The residual holes are tractable and none invalidate the overall argument: (i) the paragraph-1 / paragraph-2 internal-contradiction re: 'dome has no equation' vs 'dome's 2026 eclipse formula' is the single clearest rhetorical handle for a dome defender and should be tightened in both .finding and the first paragraph — this is the same issue WIN-068 c8 caught on the sibling WIN; (ii) the 'FSF vs shadow coverage' discriminator in paragraph 7 is mis-framed because both are already factors in the dome's formula, and the real discriminator is FSF vs magnetic-latitude scaling; (iii) the 'any smooth monotonic H(r)' claim is asserted without demonstration in the reader-facing text — it needs a footnote or a concrete demonstration against Gaussian/linear/power-law alternatives.

The three minor issues (Chapman 1933 overclaim in .finding, 0.1%/1.8% quantitative precision mismatch against independent recompute, OTS nuance in post-hoc paragraph) are debating-handle cleanups and do not affect the argument. The triple-count '69' reference is worth flagging for the decider to unify counts across Parts 2b/3.

Verdict: current_verdict_holds = TRUE with high confidence (0.88). The 'Std Model Explains / Non-discriminating' position is correctly argued. Recommended action is minor_edit to close the seven residual holes above. The review is substantially stronger after EXP-220 than it was at c5.

Strong new arguments we could add (see stronger_arguments array): (1) BOU magnetic latitude approx 48 deg is inside Kim & Chang's 30-50 deg response zone — the SIGN of the -10.9 nT anomaly is specifically predicted by standard Sq physics there; (2) pre-register the magnetic-latitude alternative for the nine 2026 stations NOW, converting 'update this WIN when 2026 data arrives' into a proper falsification setup; (3) tighten the Kp asymmetry argument to 'filter-as-ratchet' (unsettled confirms, disturbed voids).

One cross-WIN-consistency observation: with WIN-010 and WIN-068 now both conceding FSF = H(r)/r and pivoting to the training-as-test + mag-lat-vs-disc-radius critique, the sibling WIN-025 (2024 eclipse 9-station confirmation) should be checked for parallel treatment. If WIN-025 still relies on the pre-rewrite 'FSF is empirical' framing, the three-sibling cluster is inconsistent and exposes a defender attack surface. Recommend decider queue WIN-025 for re-review against the new WIN-010/068 framing.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.) — real foundational paper by Sydney Chapman; standard reference for Sq current eclipse effect
Kim & Chang (2018) Adv. Space Res. 61:2040-2054 — verified in WIN-068 chain and now shared anchor across WIN-010/025/068
INTERMAGNET BOU 2017 archive — real public resource
Dome V12 H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) — verified against raw-text/04-coordinates.txt
Dome nine-station FSF values (EBR 1.029 through COI 1.008) — verified against raw-text/03-predictions.txt exactly
Section 1.8 title 'The Coordinate Collapse: One Fix Destroys the Map' — verified against sections.json
300-6,000x more refractive than Earth's atmosphere — verified against Section 1.5 of review
citations_unchecked: monitor.py Kp < 2 gate — relied on c5 analysis; monitor.py not in raw-text/ in current clone
monitor.py hardcodes target on same commit as fetch line — relied on c5 repo-history analysis

advocate_mode.description: Dome-defender steel-man prongs against the rewritten WIN-010.

prongs.name: Internal-contradiction exploitation
prongs.defense: Paragraph 1 says 'the dome has no equation linking eclipses to magnetic perturbations'. Paragraph 2 says 'The dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF)'. These sentences contradict each other. The reviewer is being sloppy — probably the whole critique is sloppy. Dismiss.
prongs.counter: Acknowledged hole. See Hole 1 — tighten to 'no first-principles magnitude derivation', which is the intended meaning. Defender's win here is rhetorical, not substantive.

prongs.name: Conceded-derivation judo
prongs.defense: You admit FSF = H(r)/r is real with Pearson r = 0.9999. That IS a dome-geometric derivation working on real data. Your remaining objection — 'but the baseline is from observation' — applies to every calibrated physics model; standard Sq models also use measured conductivity profiles. You have proved too much.
prongs.counter: The analogy is inexact. Standard Sq models calibrate conductivity profiles against ionospheric-physics data (ionosondes, GPS/TEC, climatologies) NOT fitted to the specific eclipse magnetic signal being predicted. The dome reuses the specific 2017 BOU observation as both the counted WIN and the 2026 baseline input. Calibrating against independent reference data then predicting eclipse out-of-sample is different from using the same event as both training and test. Paragraph 3 of the new text states this distinction; a defender who reads only the headline concession misses it.

prongs.name: Straw-discriminator complaint
prongs.defense: Paragraph 7 says 2026 will test whether observations track FSF 'more tightly than shadow coverage'. But the dome's own formula is delta_Z = baseline * coverage * FSF — coverage is already in the dome's prediction. You've set up a straw man. The real discriminator you want is FSF vs magnetic-latitude scaling, which you didn't name.
prongs.counter: Valid objection. See Hole 2. The discriminator should be reframed as FSF (H(r)/r, a function of dome disc radius) vs a magnetic-latitude scaling consistent with Kim & Chang's 30-50 deg response zone.

prongs.name: Assert-without-proof complaint
prongs.defense: You say 'any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with r*tan(theta) = H(r) produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form'. You don't show this. You don't try Gaussian, linear, power-law. You assert it and move on. Prove it or drop it.
prongs.counter: Valid objection. See Hole 3. The primary critique (magnitude-anchor + training-as-test + post-hoc) survives without this argument, so either demonstrate it (do the Gaussian / linear / power-law recompute) or drop the claim.

prongs.name: Quantitative-precision challenge
prongs.defense: You claim the FSF match is 0.1% at seven stations and 1.8% at worst. My Python gets different deviations depending on which latitude table I use. Your number is not reproducible at the quoted precision.
prongs.counter: Minor. Pearson r = 0.9999 is robust to latitude source; the specific percentage deviations are sensitive to which lat/lon table is used. See Hole 4.

prongs.name: Pre-registered standard-prediction absence
prongs.defense: You say 'we could not locate a pre-registration in a timestamping venue independent of the author.' What pre-registration has standard physics produced for the 2026-08-12 eclipse? None. So you're holding the dome to a standard the mainstream doesn't meet.
prongs.counter: Standard physics isn't trying to count the 2017 BOU observation as a 'confirmed prediction'. The claim WIN-010 is making is that the dome PREDICTED the -10.9 nT before the data arrived. If the standard physics community had made the same claim, they'd face the same pre-registration requirement. Symmetric standard applied symmetrically.

advocate_mode.interpretation: The strongest defender move is the straw-discriminator complaint on paragraph 7 (defense_survives 4). Fixing Hole 2 drops this to 1 or 2. All other prongs are tractable via the holes_found recommendations.

cross_win_consistency.description: Consistency check between WIN-010 (this review) and siblings WIN-025 (2024 eclipse 9-station) and WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble).

checks.item: Kim & Chang (2018) is now the shared standard-physics anchor across WIN-010/025/068.
checks.status: WIN-010 new text: verified. WIN-068 c8 text: verified. WIN-025: NOT checked this run. Recommend decider queue WIN-025 for re-review against new framing.

checks.item: FSF = H(r)/r geometric derivation conceded in WIN-010 and WIN-068.
checks.status: WIN-010: conceded with Pearson r = 0.9999. WIN-068: conceded with parallel language. WIN-025: NOT checked.

checks.item: 'Dome has no equation' rhetorical move (the paragraph-1 / paragraph-2 contradiction).
checks.status: WIN-068 c8 caught this in .finding as moderate. WIN-010 has the same issue in .finding AND in paragraph 1 of detail_verdict_text. Fix both WIN-010 locations; WIN-068 should also be cross-checked against its own detail_verdict_text.

checks.item: Triple-count note 'three of those claims share one mechanism'.
checks.status: Present in WIN-010 new text. Not checked in WIN-068; not checked in WIN-025. If all three cite the same note, decider may want to factor it into a single prose block referenced by all three.

quantitative_verification.description: Independent recomputation of the dome's FSF values from r*tan(theta) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) and FSF = (H(r)/r) / (H(r_BOU)/r_BOU). Python computation performed in this run.
quantitative_verification.procedure: For each station, solved the transcendental r*tan(lat_deg*pi/180) = 8537*exp(-r/8619) by bisection over r in [1, 40000] km, computed H(r) = 8537*exp(-r/8619), computed H(r)/r, normalized to BOU (lat = 40.14 deg).
quantitative_verification.conclusion: FSF = H(r)/r reproduces the dome's nine published FSF values with Pearson r = 0.999922 and worst-case deviation of 2.78% at SNK. The new WIN-010 text's quantitative claim of '0.1% at seven stations, 1.8% at worst' is slightly tighter than this independent reproduction — Hole 4 flags the discrepancy. The qualitative conclusion (FSF = H(r)/r is real geometric scaling) is robust.
